coredata.dc:title: Thermal conductivity of carbon-based nanofluids; a theoretical modeling using nano-quantitative structure–property relationships
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Hamidi, Eliye
Hossein Fatemi, Mohammad
Jafari, Kimia
coredata.dc:description: Heat transfer processes account for a significant portion of the world's total energy consumption, making it crucial to enhance their efficiency. Nanofluids have garnered considerable attention in this domain as a means to achieve this objective. This study focuses on investigating the impact of key variables, including temperature, concentration, and specific surface area of graphene nanoplatelets (GNPs) dispersed in distilled water, on the thermal conductivities of these nanofluids. The quantitative structure–property relationship (QSPR) methodology is employed for this analysis. The experimental conditions in preparing GNP-based nanofluid are encoded using the quasi-SMILES notation. The calculated optimal descriptors were acquired using the Monte Carlo method in CORAL software. The results of the best model indicated that the R2 m values for all splits are more than 0.5 and ΔR2 m less than 0.2. The predictive prowess and reliability of the models were substantiated by the statistical characteristics. For the best model (split 2), these values are R2 m= 0.9290 and ΔR2 m= 0.0301, which reveals the quality of the model. The created models had a clearly defined application domain, and correlation weights were calculated for all splits. The analysis of calculated correlation weights for the developed model are used to identify the main effects of experimental variables. The obtained results indicated that high concentrations, high temperatures, and high surface areas positively influenced the thermal conductivity ratio of the investigated carbon-based nanofluids. Conversely, low temperatures and low surface areas had a negative impact on the target property.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon-based nanofluid
Thermal conductivity
Nano-quantitative structure–property relationship
quasi-SMILE
CORAL

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Cross-plot of the experimental and computed thermal conductivity values in Split 2.
Thermal conductivity ratios of GNPs with different concentrations, specific surface area and temperatures.
Identity percentages split in modeling thermal conductivities.
Statistical parameters of the developed models.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: In the above table, N is the number of compounds in each subset. R2 is the correlation coefficient, RMSE measures the average difference between values predicted by a model and the actual values, CCC is the concordance correlation coefficient, Q2 is the leave-one-out cross-validated correlation coefficient, and MAE is the mean absolute error. R2 m and ΔR2 m, determine adjacency between the predicted value with the experimental value.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The promoters of thermal conductivity of nanofluid.

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The significant promoters are highlighted by bolding.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The calculated values of the defect(Ak) for data set for split 1–4.
The calculated values of Defect(Quasi-SMILES) for TCR of carbon-based nanofluids in Split 2.

head.ce:title: Thermal conductivity of carbon-based nanofluids; a theoretical modeling using nano-quantitative structure–property relationships

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chemometrics Laboratory, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Mazandaran, Babolsar, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Chemometrics Laboratory, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Mazandaran, Babolsar, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Solar Energy Research Group, Environment and Sustainability Institute, University of Exeter, Penryn Campus, TR10 9FE, United Kingdom
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Solar Energy Research Group, Environment and Sustainability Institute, University of Exeter, Penryn Campus, TR10 9FE, United Kingdom

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Heat transfer processes account for a significant portion of the world's total energy consumption, making it crucial to enhance their efficiency. Nanofluids have garnered considerable attention in this domain as a means to achieve this objective. This study focuses on investigating the impact of key variables, including temperature, concentration, and specific surface area of graphene nanoplatelets (GNPs) dispersed in distilled water, on the thermal conductivities of these nanofluids. The quantitative structure–property relationship (QSPR) methodology is employed for this analysis. The experimental conditions in preparing GNP-based nanofluid are encoded using the quasi-SMILES notation. The calculated optimal descriptors were acquired using the Monte Carlo method in CORAL software. The results of the best model indicated that the R2 m values for all splits are more than 0.5 and ΔR2 m less than 0.2. The predictive prowess and reliability of the models were substantiated by the statistical characteristics. For the best model (split 2), these values are R2 m= 0.9290 and ΔR2 m= 0.0301, which reveals the quality of the model. The created models had a clearly defined application domain, and correlation weights were calculated for all splits. The analysis of calculated correlation weights for the developed model are used to identify the main effects of experimental variables. The obtained results indicated that high concentrations, high temperatures, and high surface areas positively influenced the thermal conductivity ratio of the investigated carbon-based nanofluids. Conversely, low temperatures and low surface areas had a negative impact on the target property.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Nanofluids have been defined using various literary expressions; however, the following definition appears to be more comprehensive: nanofluids are formed by dispersing nanoparticles with diameters ranging from 1 to 100 nm in a base fluid [1–4]. These suspensions consist of two components: the liquid base, which can be water, oils, and organic liquids such as triethylene glycol and ethylene, as well as polymer solutions and biofluids [5], and the other component consists of nanoparticles, which can be metals, oxides, carbides, and carbon nanotubes [6]. Nanofluids have recently gained significant attention and interest due to their greatly improved thermal properties, which have led to increased utilization in biomedical products, enhanced energy transfer, and intensified heat transfer in mechanical devices [7]. Since the thermophysical properties of heat-carrying fluids have the greatest impact on heating efficiency, the addition of nanoparticles to a fluid brings about changes in various parameters. These changes include heat capacity, heat transfer coefficient, thermal conductivity, nanofluid stability, viscosity, pH, and density. However, it should be noted that increased nanoparticle concentration can worsen nanofluid instability, leading to particle deposition on mechanical surfaces and subsequent damage to mechanical systems. To achieve optimal production of nanofluids with enhanced heat transfer capability, it is advisable to employ nanoparticles at low concentrations. By using particles with nanometer dimensions and extremely low concentrations, the risk of particle deposition and clogging within heating systems is typically minimized [8]. Therefore, a comprehensive understanding of the thermophysical properties of nanofluids is essential for the improved design of new materials. Evaluating the thermal conductivity as well as the chemical and physical properties of nanomaterials is crucial in nanotechnology research.

Numerous studies are focusing on the preparation and thermal conductivity values of nanofluids [9–11]. For instance, Praveen Kumar et al. [12] conducted experimental investigations on properties such as thermal conductivity, stability, and viscosity of copper nanoparticles and a mixture of fly ash-copper (FA:Cu) nanoparticles suspended in water. Another study by Alireza Akhgar et al. [13] examined the thermal conductivity of a hybrid nanofluid consisting of water-ethylene glycol/TiO2-MWCNT. The results indicated that an increase in temperature and volume fraction leads to an enhancement in the thermal conductivity of the nanofluid.

Carbon-based nanoparticles have the highest thermal conductivity among various types of nanoparticles. Therefore, more attention is given to carbon-based nanoparticles in nanofluid research [14]. The thermal conductivity and heat transfer characteristics of carbon-based nanofluids are generally higher than those of metal and metal oxide nanofluids. This is primarily attributed to the exceptional thermal conductivity of carbon-based nanomaterials. Consequently, the utilization of nanofluids, particularly carbon-based nanofluids, is considered more suitable [15]. Published reports have consistently demonstrated that carbon nanotubes (CNTs) and graphene exhibit significantly higher thermal conductivity [16] compared to other nanomaterials. In addition to their higher thermal conductivity, carbon-based nanofluids offer several other advantages. They have lower density, high aspect ratio, reduced surface corrosion and erosion, lower pressure drop, and require less pumping power [17,18]. The overall heat transfer coefficient of automobile radiators has been shown to increase using a nanofluid based on graphene nanoplatelets as a coolant [19]. A study was conducted to compare the viscosity and thermal conductivity properties of three different types of nanofluids containing graphene [20]. The thermal conductivity of suspensions containing ethylene glycol and single-walled carbon nanotubes, which determines their effective thermal conductivity, has been reported in the literature [21].

Conducting experimental studies on certain cases of these suspensions can be expensive, time-consuming, and potentially hazardous to the environment due to the release of toxic chemicals. Therefore, developing theoretical models to predict the thermophysical properties of nanofluids becomes necessary and intriguing. By developing such models, we can gain a better understanding of the influential factors on these properties and reduce the reliance on extensive experimental analyses.

The thermophysical profile of graphene oxide and MXene hybrid nanofluids for sustainable energy applications was predicted using a Bayesian optimized neural network with K–cross fold validation [22]. Model prediction with machine learning was used to enhance the thermal performance of a metal hydride reactor for hydrogen storage with graphene oxide nanofluid [23]. The thermal conductivity of a novel hybrid nanofluid was measured using a laboratory method. The obtained experimental data were then used to develop an artificial neural network (ANN) model, allowing for the estimation of thermal conductivity values [24]. The thermal conductivity of Al2O3/water (40 %)–ethylene glycol (60 %) nanofluids is estimated using an artificial neural network and correlation based on experimental data [25]. Hemmat Esfe et al. [26] investigated correlations of thermal conductivity of alumina nanoparticles dispersed in pure ethylene glycol by neural network modeling using experimental data propos. Rostami et al. [27] investigated after generating experimental data points for different volume fractions of nanoparticles and different temperatures, an algorithm to find the best neuron number in the hidden layer of artificial neural network is proposed to find the best architecture and then to predict the thermal conductivity.

There have been numerous theoretical studies utilizing artificial neural networks to investigate the thermal conductivity of nanofluids. However, there has been relatively little research conducted using the nano-quantitative structure–property relationships (nano-QSPR). The QSPR methodology is a theoretical method employed to explore, predict, and interpret the physicochemical properties of various chemicals. It involves establishing quantitative relationships between molecular structure (descriptors) as dependent variables and different properties of chemicals as independent variables.

QSPR has been the subject of numerous studies focused on modeling and predicting the biological and physicochemical properties of nanomaterials [28–30]. When applied to samples in the nano-scale, it is referred to as 'nano–QSPR' [31]. Also, the QSPR methodology is used for developing mathematical relationships between various properties of nanofluids. For example, QSPR has been used to predict the relative thermal conductivity of metal oxide nanofluids by linking their structural properties to their thermal conductivity [32]. Another study utilized QSPR to predict the thermal conductivity and viscosity of nanofluids [33]. Similarly, predictive models for the thermal conductivity of alumina and CuO–based nanofluids were developed using the same method [34].

Furthermore, to better understand factors that impact thermal conductivity properties, it is logical to utilize the QSPR model for the necessary analyses instead of relying solely on experimental analysis, which can be time-consuming, expensive, and sometimes involve toxic materials. The objective of this study is to employ the modified quasi-SMILES method, which is a tool to represent all available data from experimental data, to model and forecast the thermal conductivity of carbon nanofluids. Furthermore, the investigation includes exploring the effects of temperature, specific surface areas, and concentrations on the thermal conductivity of carbon nanofluids. While numerous studies have been conducted to model the behaviour of metal-based nanofluids, our literature surveys indicate a scarcity of reports on modeling the thermal conductivities of graphene nanoplatelet (GNP) nanofluids. Hence, this study represents the first report on the utilization of the quasi-SMILES methodology in QSPR modeling for predicting the thermal conductivities of graphene nanoplatelet nanofluids.

The collected data consists of the thermal conductivity ratio (TCR) of 72 stable homogeneous nanofluids made by adding suitable GNP in water without any surfactants. The collected dataset (reported uncertainty 5%) [35] is provided in Table 1 . The elected endpoint, TCR, is defined based on the following equation.
(1)
TCR =
Thermal conductivity of nanofluid
Thermal conductivity of pure fluid

Experimental variables are the temperature that was varied in the range of 15 to 40 °C, the concentrations of nanoparticles that were set at 0.025, 0.05, 0.075, and 0.1 wt% and three different specific surface areas of 300, 500, and 750 m2.g−1 [35]. They report 5 % uncertainty in the determination of TCR. The data set (72 data points) was divided into four subsets; the training subset (about 25 %), the invisible training collection (about 25 %), the calibration collection (about 25 %), and the validation collection (about 25 %). These data are coded using quasi-SMILES notation, and then the Monte Carlo optimization [36] is applied in CORAL software (http://www.insilico.eu/coral/). CORAL (correlation and logic) is a unique and free software that provides advanced capabilities for developing QSPR/QSAR models to select the optimal set of descriptors (scheme S1 of supplementary material).

Quasi-SMILES is a promising format for representing nanofluids in QSPR studies. The use of this structural template creates models that can account for any characteristics and conditions encoded in the quasi-SMILES structure. As noted in the previous section, experimental conditions for obtaining the thermal conductivity of the dataset are encoded by quasi-SMILES format, with selected characters encoding physicochemical conditions such as temperature, concentration and specific surface areas of GNPs, as listed in Table 2 (scheme S2 of supplementary material).

At first, all the data is distributed to the training collection (marked by +), invisible training collection (marked by −), calibration collection (marked by #), and validation collection (marked by *) randomly. Each subset plays a special task in the modeling process, as follows:
1.
The training collection is used to create a model with a target setting.
2.
The invisible training collection is used to avoid overtraining.
3.
The calibration collection is used for the optimization process.
4.
The validation collection is used to evaluate the predictability of the model.

The splitting are done randomly to compare the obtained results with each other. Then, the split with the best results is selected for further investigations. To ensure the correctness of the modeling process and avoid concerns about randomness, the percentage of identity for four quasi-SMILES splits is provided in Table 3 . Four split and disparate models are proposed on the 72 collected data.

After subsets were fully defined, the optimal descriptors were calculated to model the thermal conductivity ratio of selected nanofluids. The optimal descriptors were calculated from the following relationship (Eq. (2) created using CORAL software.
(2)
DCW(T, N) =
∑
k = 1
N T
CW
A k

In the above equation, DCW is the optimal descriptor, and T is the threshold that divides descriptors into active and passive groups. If the descriptor is active, it has a numerical value, and if it is zero, the descriptor is inactive. The number of epochs of the Monte Carlo optimization is N. With increasing number of epochs, the correlation coefficient for training and invisible sets is enhanced. Ak is the kth variables, NT is the total number of variables, and CW(Ak) is the correlation weight of Ak [37]. For example, the calculated values of CW(Ak) for different Ak in split 1, CW(%11)= 0.6048, CW(%21)= 0.0355, CW(%31)= 1.2165 and DCW= 1.7857.The general form of the proposed model is created using the optimal descriptors from the linear regression method calculated by the following equation.
(3)
Target =
C
0
+
C 1
×
DCW(T*,N*)

To assess the developed models and their predictability, some statistical parameters are used. Roy et al. [38] have proposed some metrics to validate QSPR models, two of which are R2 m and ΔR2 m, determining adjacency between the predicted TCR value with the experimental TCR value, which are obtained from Eqs. (4) and (5).
(4)
R
m
2
¯
=
r
m
2
+
r m
′ 2
2
(5)
Δ
R m 2
=
r m 2
-
r m
′ 2
that
r
m
2
=
r
2
× (1 -
r
2

r
0
2
)
and

′ 2
=
r 2
×
1 -
r

r 0
′ 2
. It should be mentioned that r2 and
r
0
2
are the determination coefficients for the least-squares regression with intercept and without intercept, respectively. For a good prediction, the value of ΔR2 m should preferably be less than 0.20, while R2 m is greater than 0.50. Furthermore, the application domain of the model should be examined.

Defect(quasi-SMILES) parameters should be computed for all data points in the data collection. With the numerical data on the defects of AK, one can compare the reliability of the prediction for quasi-SMILES. If the probability of AK in the training set is equal to the probability of AK in the calibration set, the defect is zero. This situation is ideal and does not happen normally. It is possible to obtain this amount of defects in training, calibration, test, and validation subsets. The calculation procedure for defining the application domain has been clearly discussed by Toropov et al. [39]. The measurement domain of applicability for a substance is obtained with Eq. (6).
(6)
Defect(quasi - SMILES) =
∑
S AK
- Defect
(
A k
)
Finally, quasi-SMILES fall into the domain of applicability if Eq. (7) does work for the target feature.
(7)
Defect (quasi - SMILES) < 2 ×
Defect
( q u a s i - SMILES)
¯
where
D e f e c t
quasi - S M I L E S
¯
is the average of the visible set and the Defect(quasi–SMILES) gives the possibility of defining the domain of applicability diagnoses outliers for the CORAL models.

The physicochemical properties and experimental conditions of GNP-based nanofluids are encoded by quasi-SMILES notations which are shown in Table 2. Then, the data is randomly divided into four subsets of training, invisible training, calibration and validation collections, as explained in the previous section. The splitting of data set is repeated four times to avoid any chance correlation. the percentage of similarity between these splite are calculated and shown in Table 3. As can be seen in table there is not any high similarity between them.

Models are developed by considering quasi-SMILES features as independent variables and the relative thermal conductivity (TCR) as dependent variables via the CORAL software. After completing the model optimization by Monte Carlo method according to eq. (3), the following models are obtained for four data set splits:
(8)
S p l i t
1 :
T C R
= 1.0769

0.0016 )
+
0.0834
( ±

×
D C W
( 1.4 )
(9)
S p l i t
2 :
T C R
=
1.0525
( ±
0.0011 )
+
0.0769
( ±
0.0008 )
×
D C W
( 1.4 )
(10)
S p l i t
3 :
T C R
=
1.0617
( ±
0.0028 )

0.0540

D C W
( 1.1 )
(11)
S p l i t
4 :
T C R
=
1.0677
( ±
0.0025 )

0.0571

D C W
(
1 , 1
)

To evaluate the efficiency of these models, their statistical parameters were calculated. The details are shown in Table 4 for all splits. In this Table, n represents the number of compounds in each subset, CCC is the concordance correlation coefficient, Q2 is the leave-one-out cross-validation coefficient, R2 is the correlation coefficient, and MAE is the mean absolute error, which is a measure of the differences between experimental and calculated values of TCR. The statistical parameters in Table 4 confirm that the resulting models were satisfactory for all studied splits. Moreover, the values of R2 and Q2 were both greater than 0.75, R2 m > 0.5 and ΔR2 m < 0.2 for all splits. The comparison of statistical parameters in Table 4 shows that the performance of the developed model in split 2 is better than other splits (R2 m = 0.9290 and ΔR2 m = 0.0301).

The cross-plots of split 2 are shown in Fig. 1 . These plots show the variation of experimental versus calculated values of the thermal conductivities of the studied nanofluid. As can be seen in these plots, there is a good correlation between the calculated and experimental values of TCR of the nanofluids.

Correlation weight is a main principle in the Monte Carlo method. The calculated values of correlation weights create worthwhile information about the various features, which are able to show their effect on thermal conductivity. The correlation weights extracted by the Monte Carlo method from the quasi-SMILES structures and the calculated values for each descriptor are divided into two categories; one of which is active descriptor, and the other is inactive descriptor, which do not affect the model [7,40,41]. Components whose correlation weight is positive in all investigated cases lead to an increase in the target property. In contrast, other members with a negative correlation weight, are introduced as negative promoters for the target property. Herein (shown in Table 5 ), the majority of the components had a positive effect on TCR of the studied carbon-based nanofluids. The values of %22, %23, %24 and %25 represent the high temperature condition, and the coded values of %12 and %13 represent the high concentrations of the considered nanofluids. The coded values of %31 and %32 represent the surface areas of 500 and 750 m2/g of nanoparticles, respectively. The coded values of %10 and %30 (as stable negative promoters) indicate low temperature and low surface areas, respectively. Components with different positive and negative correlations in different splits have an unclear effect on TCR.

The value of defect(Ak) for all variables are calculated and shown in Table 6 using Eq. (7). These values are used to define the applicability domain and to check the presence of any outlier data. The value of defect(quasi-SMILES) for split 2 (as the best split) is less than 0.1481
( 2 ×
Defect
quasi - SMILES
)
¯
, which indicates that there is not any outlier in the developed model. All calculated values are provided in Table 7 . Accordingly, the efficacy of the applied methodology based on the optimized quasi-SMILES descriptors was validated; subsequently, a discussion on the mechanistic interpretation and the scope of application for the constructed models took place; ultimately, the proposed approach yields models consistent with OECD(Organization for Economic Co-operation and Development) principles [32]. We think the proposed technique would be accurately practical for more types of nanofluids and endpoints. The presence of a nanolayer at the solid–liquid interface of GNPs and the aggregation of nanoparticles are considered a significant process contributing to the enhancement of thermal conductivity in nanofluids [35]. The liquid molecules are adjacent to the particle surfaces are recognized for their formation of layered structures, resembling the behaviour of solids. The results reveal that the GNPs exhibiting higher specific surface area and higher particle concentrations demonstrate superior thermal conductivity. Nanofluids containing GNPs with low concentrations are suitable for applications at moderate temperatures, such as solar collectors.

The relative thermal conductivities of carbon-based nanofluids were predicted using their quasi-SMILES notation in CORAL software. The dataset contains 72 data points, which were obtained at different temperatures, concentrations, and specific surfaces of GNPs. To develop nano-QSPR models for the thermal conductivity ratio, the Monte Carlo optimization method was used and the experimental conditions were coded by quasi-SMILES method. To ensure the modeling process's accuracy and avoid doubts about the randomness of the proposed models, the primary data set was divided into four subsets. The specifications of the proposed models were fully calculated, and the results indicated that the developed models were statistically significant for predicting the thermal conductivity ratios of carbon-based nanofluids in the applicability domain model. The best model was found in Split 2 with R2 m= 0.9290 and ΔR2 m= 0.0301, while the weakest statistical performance was observed in Split 4 with R2 m= 0.6509 and ΔR2 m= 0.1890. The application domain of the created models was clearly defined, and correlation weights were calculated for all splits. The results showed that high concentrations, high temperatures, and high surface area have a positive effect on the thermal conductivity of studied carbon-based nanofluid. In contrast, low temperatures and low surface areas have a negative effect on the target property. The statistical performance parameters of the models were satisfactory. The results suggested that GNPs with higher surface area and higher particle concentrations exhibit higher thermal conductivity. However, for medium-temperature applications such as collectors and thermal systems, it is possible to achieve acceptable thermal conductivity by utilizing a lower concentration of GNPs.

Eliye Hamidi: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Software, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Mohammad Hossein Fatemi: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Project administration. Kimia Jafari: Writing – review & editing, Methodology, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cplett.2024.141344.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1
